system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: Melodic Target Notes and the Narrative of the Solo
Pages 24-25 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

                           Example n. 6: Altered line using the shape
He was exploring the shape, being as flexible as possible, finding different intervallic
combinations, and different ways to resolve the line.

An important disclaimer regarding these concepts is that while they are valuable for expanding
and developing melodic ideas, it is crucial to work on internalizing and integrating this new
material. Bernstein emphasizes the concept of flexibility once again, highlighting the need to be
comfortable and adaptable with the ideas, being able to play them in different ways. He suggests
using fingerings that feel natural and comfortable for us, as trying to execute ideas with awkward
fingerings can hinder the process of internalization. In my experience observing Bernstein's
playing, I've noticed his remarkable flexibility with fingerings. He effortlessly switches between
multiple options and selects the most appropriate one for the musical context.

Moreover, Bernstein emphasizes the importance of being flexible in the delivery of ideas to
ensure they feel organic within the flow of a solo. He likens it to a conversation, where you don't
want to be preoccupied with when to interject something you've just learned. Such distractions
can hinder your ability to listen to what's happening and may result in untimely or forced
delivery of the idea. He suggests that through practicing these techniques, you develop the
necessary flexibility to internalize new ideas and seamlessly integrate them into musical dialogue
with authenticity and meaning.

Bernstein also emphasizes the distinction between playing ideas that relate to each other and
create a narrative with purpose versus merely playing notes that work over the chords. He
acknowledges that some players have built careers based solely on the latter approach.
Ultimately, he believes that the essence of music lies in weaving our own stories within the
context of a song. He asserts that the true depth and meaning in music come not from playing
more notes or complex passages, but from testing how our personal stories resonate within the
world of stories. This is an aspect that formal education cannot provide—it requires the intention
to play with others and explore our musical ideas in the real world. In essence, his goal is to
illuminate how the language of music functions, how ideas are constructed, and how to skillfully
combine them. By doing so, he aims to empower us to develop our own artistic craftsmanship, to
create our own ideas, and ultimately to tell our own stories.



                                                                                                24


Melodic target notes

Bernstein consistently emphasizes the concept of using the target notes of the melody as the basis
for melodic improvisation whenever we play a tune. A notable example he mentions is the song
"Darn That Dream," which holds significant importance in understanding these target notes as
they form the core of the song's structure.




                        Example n. 1: Target notes for “Darn that dream”


Once you feel comfortable with the original target notes derived from the melody, he suggests
creating your own. According to him, these landing points are closely connected to the notes he
chooses for the top voicings while comping. This is because those notes often embody strong and
melodically pleasing phrases. For the initial bars of "Darn That Dream," Bernstein proposes the
following target notes:




                  Example n. 1: Bernstein’s target notes for “Darn that dream”


Once you can identify these notes, he encourages exploring melodic lines that lead to these target
notes, starting with simple ideas and later exploring more complex ones, imparting a narrative
quality and providing a specific framework for storytelling during improvisation. This exercise
serves as a natural extension of practicing lines that resolve on each degree of the chord scale,
but with the melody guiding us to the precise target notes within the chord progression.


                                                                                               25


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
